coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots: A selective detection of Fe3+ and understanding its mechanism
coredata.dc:creator: Ali, Mir Sahanur
Bhunia, Nayan
Ali, Mir Sahidul
Karmakar, Srikanta
Mukherjee, Prasun
Chattopadhyay, Dipankar
coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based sensor has been built many times. Optical properties, such as optical absorption, have been neglected for too long. Here, we have reported the luminescence of blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with average particle size 3–6 nm, synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, which give intense blue emission under 340 nm excitation wavelengths. As the fluorescence of N-CQDs can be efficiently quenched by Fe3+, the N-CQDs can be constructed as a sensor system for Fe3+ with good sensitivity and selectivity. Effective detection and quantification of Fe3+ at as little as 0.07 µM and 0.22 µM is achieved.
coredata.dcterms:subject: N-doped carbon quantum dots
Fe3+ ion sensing
Limit of detection
Security ink

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) by using N-CQDs
Real application of N-CQD as security ink

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Represents the (a) TEM image, (b) HRTEM image, (c) SAED pattern, and (d) Histogram plot of the sized distribution of the synthesized N-CQDs.

(a) UV–Vis. absorption (black line: left axis) spectrum and PL emission (red line right axis) at λex = 340 nm spectrum of N-CQDs, (b) PL emission spectra (absorption corrected) of N-CQDs at different excitation wavelength (280 nm to 380 nm with an interval of 20 nm), (c) and (d) Raman and FTIR spectra of N-CQDs, respectively. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
(a) and (b) shows the changes of UV–visible absorbance and PL emission intensity of N-CQDs (50 mgL-1) in addition with various 10 mM metal ions (Na+, K+, Ca2+, Fe3+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Sn2+).

(a) UV–visible absorption of N-CQDs changes in the presence of different Fe3+ ions concentration, (b) PL emission (absorption corrected) intensity of N-CQDs changes on different Fe3+ ions concentration, (c) Deviation of UV–visible absorbance (A-A0) with Fe3+ ions’ concentration, inset indicates its linear variation (0–2 μM), (d) PL quenching (I0-I) with Fe3+ ions’ concentration, inset demonstrates its linear variation (0–2 μM).

(upper part) Schematic for N-CQDs fluorescence quenched by Fe3+ ions (a) TEM image of the N-CQDs, (b) SEM image of Fe3+-induced N-CQDs aggregation, (c) FTIR spectra and (d) TRPL of N-CQDs and N-CQDs + 10 mM Fe3+with an excitation wavelength of λex ∼ 300 nm.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent N-doped carbon quantum dots: A selective detection of Fe3+ and understanding its mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Technology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700009, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Polymer Science and Technology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700009, West Bengal, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700106, West Bengal, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology, University of Calcutta, Kolkata 700106, West Bengal, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) based sensor has been built many times. Optical properties, such as optical absorption, have been neglected for too long. Here, we have reported the luminescence of blue-emitting nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) with average particle size 3–6 nm, synthesized via a simple hydrothermal method, which give intense blue emission under 340 nm excitation wavelengths. As the fluorescence of N-CQDs can be efficiently quenched by Fe3+, the N-CQDs can be constructed as a sensor system for Fe3+ with good sensitivity and selectivity. Effective detection and quantification of Fe3+ at as little as 0.07 µM and 0.22 µM is achieved.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heavy metal ion detection is a vital and burning issue because of water pollution, and the heavy metal ions can accumulate in the soft tissues of biological things present in the environment, where they can cause a variety of acute and long-term toxicities that can harm various body organs. Iron is one of the heavy elements which mainly cause water contamination majorly. The enzymatic catalysis, cellular metabolism, RNA and DNA synthesis and repair, oxygen transport in hemoglobin, and other functions are all facilitated by Fe3+ ions [1]. Thus iron also can result in, e.g., anemia, diabetes, breathing issues, liver, heart, and kidney damage, as well as several health ailments [1,2]. Also, the condition/performance of various organs, including the heart, pancreas, and lungs, can be negatively impacted by an excess Fe3+ ion, which can also cause some malignancies [1–3]. Fe3+ detection significantly impacts biological systems and must be very sensitive, selective, cost-effective, and ultrafast. Still, numerous methods exist for detecting Fe3+ ions, like electrochemical and fluorescence spectroscopy, colorimetry, mass spectrometry, and atomic absorption spectroscopy [2]. Among them, fluorescence and absorption spectroscopy is globally used due to their fast detection, cost-effectiveness, and ease of handling [2].

Over the past few decades, quantum dots have gained a lot of attention among researchers due to their tremendous possibilities for utilization in various fields. Kavitha Shivaji et al. had used CdS QD in therapeutic applications in lung cancer cells [4]. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), which offer several benefits over semiconductor quantum dots, have drawn a lot of attention as a research topic to explore. Environmental issues and biological toxicity limit the use of conventional semiconductor-based metal QDs. The new class of carbon nanomaterials included fluorescent carbon nanoparticles or CQDs offer, a suitable alternative. They do this by virtue of their superior tuneable optical characteristics, photoluminescence stability, biocompatibility, and chemical inertness. They pose a number of outstanding properties, including high photoluminescence (PL) emission, low toxicity, excellent chemical stability, environmental friendliness, and broadband optical absorption. Their key advantage is their highly cost-effective mass production of CQDs. Though CQDs are a vastly studied material, the fluorescence mechanism of CQDs is still provocative [5,6]. CQDs are amorphous to crystalline carbon-based that typically quasi-spherical quantum dots. It is generally composed of sp2-graphitic carbon or graphene and sheets of graphene oxide joined with sp3-hybridized carbon, which has fluorescence capabilities. The quantum confinement effect, surface state, molecule state, and crosslink-enhanced emission are the four possible fluorescence mechanisms for CQDs, according to Zhu et al. [7]. According to Li et al. isolated sp2-conjugated carbon clusters promote CQDs' fluorescence properties. [8]. Various methods had been deployed for synthesizing CQDs such as electrochemical method, laser ablation, hydrothermal/solvothermal, microwave radiation etc. Hydrothermal processis one of the described methods for synthesizing CQDs, and it has a low price and a high output yield and have not require any solvent. Deng et al. had fabricated CQDs by alcohol using three-electrode system. [9]. Chen's group has synthesized blue-emitting CQDs by pyrolyzing hair in one step at 300 °C in nitrogen environment [10]. However, high temperatures and inert gases are necessary and production yield is also low. Among all method hydrothermal method is simple and low cost method. Therefore a quick and easy thermal treatment technique for synthesizing CQDs is highly desired.

In this work, we have successfully synthesized highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped CQDs (N-CQDs) from citric acid by single-step hydrothermal method. SEM, EDAX, TEM, XRD, FTIR, and fluorescence spectroscopy were used to evaluate the optical and structural characteristics of N-CQDs. The average particle size of synthesized N-CQDs is 3–6 nm which is much smaller than some previous reports [11]. Excitation wavelength dependent emission behaviour, good photostability, and high stability were all exhibited in the as-prepared N-CQDs with a high quantum yield (QY) of 12.55%. We also looked at the fluorescence properties of N-CQDs in the presence of metal ions. It was noticed that the fluorescence of N-CQDs could be selectively quenched in the presence of Fe3+ ions and that the quenching rate showed a strong linear relationship with the concentration of Fe3+ ions in the range of 40 nM to 10 nM. The metal ion selectivity of N-CQDs in an aqueous solution was investigated using other coexisting distinct metal ions. From the results, it can be concluded that the N-CQDs have massive potential for use in the field of Fe3+ ion sensing. Effective limit of detection (LOD) and limit of quantitation (LOQ) of Fe3+ at as little as 0.07 µM and 0.22 µM are achieved through the prepared N-CQDs with 80% photostability after 4 h continuous UV (365 nm) illumination. The majority of research work has attributed the N-CQDs phenolic hydroxyl groups, which selectively identify Fe3+ and cause fluorescence quenching.

Sodium chloride (NaCl, 99%), potassium chloride (KCl, 98.5%), calcium chloride (CaCl2, 95%), ferric chloride anhydrous (FeCl3·H2O, 98%), nickel chloride hexahydrate (NiCl2·6H2O, 97%), cobalt chloride hexahydrate (CoCl2·6H2O, 99%), stannous chloride dihydrate (SnCl2·2H2O, 99%), ethylenediamine (≥99%) and citric acid were purchased from Merck India Limited. Poly (vinyl alcohol) (MW-115000, PVA) was purchased from Loba Chemicals. All compounds were of analytical grade and used without additional purification.Throughout the experiment, deionized (DI) water was used.

All glassware was cleaned very carefully, and ensured that there was no contamination prior to use. At first, 1 g of citric acid was dissolved in 60 ml DI water by magnetic stirring. After that, 350 µl ethylenediamine was added into that solution and stirred for 40 min, and then the solution was put into a Teflon-lined autoclave and heated for 5 h at 200 °C. After the reaction, the mixture was cooled to room temperature, the resulting solution was centrifuged to remove larger particles, and the final product was filtered using a dialysis membrane. The whole experiment is schematically depicted in Scheme 1 .

To investigate the selectivity of N-CQDs on Fe3+ions, N-CQDs (50 μg mL−1 were mixed with a series of metal ions (10 mM) (Na+, K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Sn2+, and Fe3+)) respectively. Using PL and UV–Vis spectrophotometers with 340 nm excitation wavelengths, respectively, the PL and UV–vis spectra of the N-CQDs-metal mixture were measured.

The mixture of N-CQDs (50 μg mL−1) and various concentrations of Fe3+ (from 40 nM to 10 mM) were prepared in DI water. The PL and UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded in Horiba Fluorolog 3–22 luminescence spectrometer and Perkin Elmer Lambda25 spectrophotometer, respectively.

The optical characterization of the synthesized N-CQDs was done by several techniques, UV–Vis absorption (UV–vis absorption spectrometer: PerkinElmer model: lambda25), photoluminescence (PL spectrofluorimeter: Horiba Fluorolog 3–22 luminescence spectrometer) emission, Raman spectrum (Renishaw inVia Raman spectrometer), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) PerkinElmer spectrometer: spectrum 2). The morphology of the N-CQDs were taken by scanning electron microscope (SEM: ZEISS EVO-MA10, Germany) and transmission electron microscope (TEM: JEOLJEM 2100) whereas energy dispersive x-ray (EDX) analysis and elemental mapping were done to find the elemental composition in N-CQDs. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) experiment was done to find out the hydrodynamic size by Malvern Zetasizer Ver. 6.00. The time-resolved PL spectra wererecorded by lifetime spectrometer (HORIBA Scientific Deltaflex modular fluorescence PPO 650).

Under UV irradiation, the N-CQDs suspended in water showed intense bluish fluorescence emission. Fig. 2
a represents the absorption spectra of as-prepared N-CQDs. Two peaks are observed in the UV–vis spectrum: a very strong peak at 230 nm and a smaller peak at 338 nm. The origin of these two peaks can be explained as follows; one peak is due to the π-conjugated sp2 carbon core, which is attributed to the π- π* transition of electrons, and since there is some functional group attached to the sp2 hybridized carbon core, therefore, n-π* electronic transitions take place in non-bonding orbital [17–22]. The digital image of N-CQDs dispersed in DI water under normal light, and UV light was presented in the inset of Fig. 2 a. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibited excitation wavelength dependent photoluminescence emission characteristics. The excitation wavelength dependent PL spectra are presented in Fig. 2 b; the PL peak emission intensity increased with an excitation wavelength and reached its maximum at an excitation wavelength of 340 nm, after which it dropped as the excitation wavelength increased further. The synthesized N-CQDs showed good photostability, PL emission retain ∼ 74% after 4 h of 365 nm UV illumination (S3a, S3b) and quantum yield of as-synthesized N-CQDs is ∼ 12.55%. The Raman spectra of N-CQDs taken at 532 nm excitation wavelength are depicted in Fig. 2 c. Raman spectrum showed two distinct peaks at 1365 cm−1 corresponding to D-band (sp3 hybridization), which represents the defect amount and functionalization. In the Raman spectrum, the peak at 1600 cm−1 corresponds to the G-band (sp2 hybridization), representing the amount of graphitization associated with the N-CQDs [16,19,23]. The intensity ratio of the G band's peak to the D band's peak was found to be 1.71; this higher intensity ratio indicates that the prepared N-CQDs are composed of graphitic crystalline structures [18].This intensity ratio of the G band to the D band is greater than the intensity ratio of N-CQDs synthesized from natural carbon sources [24,25].

To know about surface functionality, N-CQDs are characterized by the FTIR spectrum. Performed FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2 d) shows absorption bands centered at 3438 cm−1, weak absorption bands around 2845 cm−1, and broad absorption bands at 1383 cm−1, which correspond to O − H, C − H, and − COOH bond vibration, respectively which indicates that N − CQDs may contain surface moieties connected to their surface [13,19,20,26,27]. A sharp absorption band of C = C at around 1631 cm−1 is observed, indicating that N-CQDs are graphitic [27,28]. Also, the C − O bond stretching at about 1027 cm−1 and peak at 600 cm−1 correspond to C − N bending, and a weak absorption band of N − H at 2925 cm−1 is observed [13,19,20]. The FTIR results indicate that the surface of synthesized N-CQDs contains carboxyl, carbonyl, and hydroxyl group (oxygen-containing functional group) and sp2 aromatic structural units [18].

The sensing behaviour of N-CQDs in the presence of different metal ions such as Na+, K+, Ni2+, Co2+, Ca2+, Sn2+, and Fe3+ (chloride salts) was examined through the PL spectrophotometer, and it was found to be sensitive and selective to Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence of N-CQDs was turn-off by Fe3+ ion which is shown in the inset of Fig. 3
a.

Various metal ions (Na+, K+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Co2+, Sn2+and Fe3+) were added to N-CQDs aqueous solution (50 μgmL−1), respectively. It is apparent that in the presence of Fe3+, PL intensity of N-CQDs was significantly quenched instantly (Fig. 3 a); by contrast, other metals have negligible influence on the N-CQDs luminescence effect. The histogram showing the relative variations of PL emission intensity I/I0 for different metal ions are shown in Fig. 3 b. It is observed that the PL intensity of N-CQDs significantly drops after the addition of Fe3+, while other metals showed no significant response. Therefore it can be concluded that the synthesized N-CQD is highly sensitive to Fe3+ ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of ferric ions (Fe3+) by using N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para:
Due to its critical significance in oxygen metabolism, oxygen transport, electron transfer, and catalytic processes, Fe3+ plays a significant part in our life [20]. Carbon fluorescent dots, which exhibited good sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+ metal ions, have recently been employed as fluorescent probes for heavy metal ions. The Fe3+ ions used to quench the N-CQDs luminescence were the foundation for the PL probe of the N-CQDs. The excitation wavelength was adjusted to 340 nm for all measurements, and all detections were carried out in an aqueous solution at room temperature. Fe3+ ion concentrations ranging from 0 to 10 mmol/L were taken into consideration then within a minute of introducing N-CQDs to the various amounts of Fe3+ ions, all measurements were carried out. The PL was noticed to be gradually quenching, and a simultaneous increase in UV–Vis absorption intensity (Fig. 4
a, b) was also observed on successive addition of Fe3+ solution into the N-CQD-based sensing system.

Further, the huge fluorescence property of N-CQDs played a crucial role in investigating the efficiency of N-CQDs on Fe3+ metal ions sensing. A substantial reduction in PL emission intensity was observed. A maximum PL emission quenching (∼84%) of the aqueous N-CQDs loaded with Fe3+ has been detected to that of the bare aqueous N-CQDs. Fig. 4 c displays the UV–vis absorption A-A0 progressively enhances and then became flat, and Fe3+ concentration (0–2 μM) has the best linear relationship (inset of Fig. 4 c, A–A0 = 56.01[Fe3+]-2.95, R2 = 0.99) and the LOD and LOQ is 0.36 µM and 1.09 µM respectively. Simultaneously, the higher the Fe3+ concentration, the fainter the N-CQDs luminescence shown in Fig. 4 d; this is easily seen with the naked eye when exposed to UV light. The I0-I became flat when the Fe3+ concentration was over 5 mM. In this concentration region, there are linear regions of 0–2 μM with good linear correlation (inset of Fig. 4 d, I0 –I = 37.69[Fe3+] + 183.72, R2 = 0.99), and the LOD and LOQ are 0.07 µM and 0.22 µM respectively.

The static quenching effect (SQE) and the dynamic quenching effect (DQE) are the two main methods for describing PL quenching. We assumed that Fe3+ coordinates with phenolic hydroxyl groups on the surface of N-CQDs to bridge near by N-CQDs together (Fig. 5
a and 5b) [28]. The cause of the fluorescence quenching can be explained by the unique direct interaction of Fe3+ ions and the phenolic hydroxyl groups of the N-CQDs or by the transfer process of electron energy [22]. This changewas further supported by variations in the FT-IR spectra of N-CQDs before and after adding Fe3+. The addition of Fe3+ specifically changed numerous peaks at approximately 1434 cm−1 and 1339 cm−1 into a strong narrow peak at 1380 cm−1 (Fig. 5c); we attributed this to Fe3+ ions attaching to surface hydroxyl groups due to their affinity. When Fe3+ is kept into bare aqueous N-CQDs, the existed electrons in the higher excited state (S1) of the N-CQDs force to jump in the partially filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ [28]. These transported electrons cannot come back to the ground state because of the half-filled 3d orbital of Fe3+ions. As a result, the electron-hole recombination annihilation and non-radiative fluorescence quenching (static quenching) was observed. These findings show that Fe3+ ions can be detected using the synthesized N-CQDs via aggregation (upper part of Fig. 5) induced fluorescence quenching. The synthesized N-CQDs are monodisperse highly fluorescent quantum dots with a near-spherical morphology sized between 3 and 6 nm, according to the TEM of Fig. 5 a. Large aggregation area is found in the SEM image of N-CQDs after the addition of Fe3+ (Fig. 5 b) ions to a well-dispersed N-CQDs solution and simultaneously the fluorescence of N-CQDs has been turned off.

The PL decay curves of N-CQDs in the presence of Fe3+was investigated. The time-resolved PL (TRPL) emission spectra of N-CQDs and Fe3+ions induced N-CQD with two varying concentrations (160 µM and 10 mM) has been shown in Fig. 5 d. The TRPL decay curves can be fitted by tri-exponential equation and the average fluorescence lifetime (τave ) is calculated by equation below [29,30].
(1)
y = A exp
- t /
τ 1
+ B exp
- t /
τ 2
+ C exp
- t /
τ 3

The fit of the tri-exponential function (Rtri 2 = 0.999 ˃Rbi 2 = 0.998) of TRPL decay curves suggested the presence of three different emission sites with average lifetimes of (5.89 ± 0.40), (4.90 ± 0.43), and (3.50 ± 0.33) ns, with chi-square value 1.02, 1.07 and 1.03, respectively. The average lifetime of N-CQDs is found to be 5.89 ± 0.40 ns. The average lifetime of fluorescent N-CQDs is 5.89 ns, which was higher than average lifetime of N-CQDs loaded with 10 mM Fe3+(3.50 ns). This indicates that PL emission quenching in addition with Fe3+ could be ascribed to dynamic quenching from Fe3+ions induced aggregation of N-CQDs [29].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Real application of N-CQD as security ink
ce:section.ce:para: We showed how well N-CQDs ink performed in real-world scenarios, where PVA and N-CQDs were combined with an aqueous ink, using the superior photoluminescence behaviour indicated above as foundation. Therefore, this invisible ink can be used to write on substrate for security purposes. The image (lower part of Fig. 5) illustrates the effect of UV radiation on fluorescence. The right side of the image below indicates the fluorescence quenching of N-CQDs in the solid state by Fe3+ ion, indicating that Fe3+ can be detected by solid-state spectroscopy.

In conclusion, we have described a straightforward hydrothermal method for synthesizing N-CQDs, a carbon-based fluorescent nanostructure, from citric acid. We build dual signaling probes based on N-CQDs (PL and UV absorption), with a very low limit of detection of 0.07 µM. The observed variation in the light emission intensity of the synthesized PVA-CQDs film under UV illuminations suggests that the synthesized CQDs may, in the future, be employed as a luminous solid-state sensor. Based on our findings, new opportunities for the development of NCQDs-based PL/UV absorption dual-signal probes for environmental analysis may be possible.

Mir Sahanur Ali: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing, Visualization. Nayan Bhunia: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Mir Sahidul Ali: Investigation, Data curation, Formal analysis. Srikanta Karmakar: Supervision, Conceptualization, Methodology, Visualization. Prasun Mukherjee: Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing. Dipankar Chattopadhyay: Visualization, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We are thankful to the Centre for Research in Nanoscience and Nanotechnology (CRNN), University of Calcutta, for their technical support. Srikanta Karmakar is thankful to UGC Dr. D. S. Kothari Post-Doctoral Fellowship for sponsoring the research work.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.cplett.2023.140574.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:

X. Zhu, Z. Zhang, Z. Xue, C. Huang, Y. Shan, C. Liu, T. Wang, Analytical Chemistry, 89 (2017) 12054-12058, https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.7b02499.
